heroOverview: Gastroschisis is a congenital abdominal wall defect in which the intestines, and sometimes other organs, protrude through a small full-thickness opening to the right of the umbilicus, without a protective covering membrane. It occurs in approximately 4–5 per 10,000 live births globally, with incidence rising over recent decades especially among young mothers under 20 years of age. Unlike omphalocele, the exposed bowel in gastroschisis is not covered by a sac, leaving it directly bathed in amniotic fluid that progressively damages the intestinal wall. With modern neonatal surgery, survival exceeds 90% in high-income settings, but outcomes depend heavily on the degree of bowel injury, the presence of associated intestinal atresia, and the speed of surgical repair after birth.

keyStats.prevalence: 4–5 per 10,000 live births; incidence has roughly doubled in many countries since the 1980s
keyStats.demographics: More common when the mother is under 20 years old; no strong sex predilection in the newborn
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital — present at birth; diagnosed prenatally in most high-income countries via second-trimester ultrasound

definition: Gastroschisis (ICD-10: Q79.3) is a full-thickness paraumbilical defect of the anterior abdominal wall, typically 2–4 cm wide and located immediately to the right of a normally inserted umbilical cord. The herniated viscera — most often small bowel, and sometimes large bowel, stomach, or bladder — protrude freely into the amniotic cavity without any sac, membrane, or peritoneal covering. This distinguishes gastroschisis from omphalocele, in which herniated organs are enclosed within a sac formed by the amnion and peritoneum at the base of the umbilical cord. The precise embryological origin of gastroschisis is debated; leading theories implicate disruption of the right omphalomesenteric or right umbilical vein during the fourth to fifth week of gestation, weakening the developing abdominal wall. Because the bowel floats in amniotic fluid from early in gestation, it sustains a progressive chemical serosal injury that produces thickening, matting, and the characteristic 'peel' — a fibrous coating that impairs peristalsis. Gastroschisis falls under neonatal surgery, pediatric surgery, and genetics for counselling. The condition is usually sporadic, though young maternal age, maternal smoking, use of recreational drugs such as cocaine or methamphetamine, aspirin or ibuprofen in early pregnancy, and low pre-pregnancy body mass index are consistently identified risk factors. Chromosomal anomalies are rare, occurring in under 5% of cases, contrasting with the much higher rate seen in omphalocele.

typesClassification.description: The bowel is herniated without atresia, necrosis, or perforation. This is the most common form and is associated with the best outcomes, with survival above 95% in experienced centers.

Associated with intestinal atresia, volvulus, perforation, or necrosis affecting a segment of bowel. Complex cases carry a higher risk of short-bowel syndrome and prolonged parenteral nutrition dependence.
A large defect in which the majority of the intestine, stomach, and sometimes liver herniate outside the body; rare, and associated with greater physiological stress at repair.
A rare variant in which the defect has spontaneously closed in utero, trapping the bowel outside; it carries the highest risk of severe short-bowel syndrome or stillbirth.

primarySymptoms: Herniated bowel visible outside the abdominal wall at birth, to the right of the umbilical cord, without any covering membrane or sac.
Exposed intestinal loops that appear thickened, edematous, and may be matted together due to amniotic fluid exposure throughout gestation.
A characteristic fibrous 'peel' coating the bowel surface, resulting from prolonged inflammatory damage by amniotic fluid.
Absent or minimal bowel sounds in the early neonatal period due to bowel wall edema and dysmotility.
Distended or shortened loops of bowel depending on whether atresia or volvulus is present in complex cases.
Hypothermia and fluid loss in the newborn from evaporation through the exposed, unprotected bowel surface.
Signs of dehydration and electrolyte imbalance if repair is delayed — reflected in poor skin turgor, sunken fontanelle, and laboratory abnormalities.
In complex cases, a segment of frankly ischemic or necrotic bowel may be visible, appearing dark or discolored.
Prenatal ultrasound findings include 'floating' free bowel loops in the amniotic fluid and absence of a covering membrane, distinguishing it from omphalocele.
Polyhydramnios detected on prenatal ultrasound in approximately 20–25% of affected pregnancies, reflecting impaired fetal swallowing.
earlyWarningSigns: Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein elevation in the first or second trimester, which often triggers detailed ultrasound assessment.
Free-floating bowel loops seen on routine second-trimester fetal ultrasound without a covering sac.
Increased dilation of bowel loops on serial prenatal ultrasound, suggesting developing obstruction or volvulus.
Slowing of fetal growth or oligohydramnios near term, which may indicate bowel compromise.
emergencySigns: Frankly ischemic or black-appearing bowel at birth, indicating vascular compromise requiring emergency surgery.
Inability to reduce the herniated bowel into the abdomen due to marked abdominal domain loss or severe bowel edema.
Respiratory distress and abdominal compartment syndrome after primary fascial closure, with rising ventilatory pressures and oliguria.
Sepsis signs — fever, hypotension, poor perfusion — in the first days of life, which can follow bowel perforation or infection.
Persistent failure to pass meconium or establish enteral feeds beyond six to eight weeks, raising concern for unrecognised atresia.

causes.cause: Disruption of the right omphalomesenteric artery
causes.description: The most widely accepted theory holds that involution or disruption of the right omphalomesenteric artery during the fourth to fifth week of embryogenesis weakens the right paraumbilical abdominal wall, allowing the physiologically herniated intestinal loop to remain outside rather than returning to the peritoneal cavity.

causes.cause: Failure of normal lateral body folding
causes.description: An alternative embryological model proposes that incomplete fusion of the cephalic, caudal, and lateral folds during the fourth week of development creates a persistent defect in the anterior abdominal wall; this theory accounts for the rarer midline and left-sided variants.

Cocaine, methamphetamine, and pseudoephedrine — all vasoconstrictive substances — have been associated with a two- to fourfold increased risk of gastroschisis in multiple case-control studies, consistent with the vascular disruption hypothesis.

causes.cause: Maternal NSAID use in early pregnancy
causes.description: Aspirin and ibuprofen taken during the first trimester have been linked to gastroschisis risk in several studies, possibly because prostaglandin inhibition alters fetal vascular tone and gut development during a critical window.

Mothers under 20 years of age have a risk approximately five to seven times higher than women aged 25–29; the biological mechanism is unclear but nutritional deficiencies, lower preconception weight, and higher rates of certain exposures may contribute.

Cigarette smoking in the first trimester is associated with a roughly 1.5- to 2-fold increased risk, plausibly through nicotine-induced vasoconstriction and oxidative stress during critical abdominal wall development.

Being underweight before conception is independently associated with gastroschisis risk; proposed mechanisms include suboptimal nutritional availability for tissue formation during the embryological window.

riskFactors.description: The strongest single risk factor; the exact biological mechanism is not established, but it accounts for a disproportionate share of cases in epidemiological data.
First-trimester smoking approximately doubles the risk; cessation before conception or in early pregnancy can reduce this excess risk.
Strong vasoconstrictive substances increase gastroschisis risk two- to fourfold and are among the most actionable avoidable exposures.
Aspirin and ibuprofen taken in the first trimester have been associated with gastroschisis; paracetamol is the recommended analgesic in early pregnancy for this reason.
Underweight status before pregnancy is independently associated with elevated risk, supporting preconception nutritional optimisation.

riskFactors.factor: Nitrate and nitrite exposure in diet or water
riskFactors.description: Some epidemiological studies have found associations between elevated nitrate in drinking water and gastroschisis, though the evidence is less consistent than for other risk factors.
Certain periconceptional infections have been reported as risk factors in case-control studies, possibly through inflammatory disruption of early organogenesis.

affectedDemographics: Neonates born to mothers under 20 years of age, who account for a disproportionate share of cases
Infants of mothers with low preconception body weight
Newborns in settings with high rates of maternal smoking or recreational drug use
Males and females are approximately equally affected
All ethnic groups, though incidence patterns vary by region and maternal age distribution
diagnosisOverview: Gastroschisis is diagnosed prenatally in over 80% of cases in countries with routine antenatal ultrasound. The defining sonographic finding — free-floating bowel loops in the amniotic fluid without a covering membrane, with a normally positioned umbilical cord insertion — is reliably seen from the 12th week of gestation and is unambiguous by the second trimester. Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein is markedly elevated (typically >4 multiples of the median) because fetal proteins leak directly through the exposed bowel wall. Amniocentesis is generally not indicated because the rate of chromosomal anomalies with isolated gastroschisis is below 5%, compared with over 40% in omphalocele. Serial ultrasound examinations every two to four weeks are performed after diagnosis to monitor bowel dilation, bowel wall echogenicity, amniotic fluid volume, and fetal growth, as these features predict neonatal outcome. Bowel dilation with an intraabdominal diameter above 10–17 mm on prenatal ultrasound correlates with longer time to full enteral feeding after repair. MRI may supplement ultrasound in equivocal cases or when liver herniation is suspected. At birth, the diagnosis is immediately apparent on inspection; postnatal assessment includes a chest radiograph, echocardiogram to exclude congenital heart defects that co-occur in approximately 5–10% of cases, and laboratory work including complete blood count, electrolytes, blood gas, and cross-match. The bowel is assessed for atresia, volvulus, and viability.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary method for prenatal diagnosis, identifying free-floating bowel loops without a sac alongside a normally inserting umbilical cord.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The mother lies on her back while the sonographer applies gel and scans the abdomen; the procedure takes 20–45 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated AFP in maternal blood (typically >4 MoM) raises suspicion for open fetal defects including gastroschisis and prompts detailed ultrasound.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is drawn from the mother's arm, usually between 15 and 20 weeks' gestation; results are available within a few days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tracks bowel dilation, bowel wall changes, and fetal growth every 2–4 weeks after diagnosis to guide timing and mode of delivery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Repeated standard ultrasound examinations during the third trimester.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides additional detail about bowel anatomy and organ involvement when ultrasound findings are equivocal or when liver herniation is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The mother lies in an MRI scanner for approximately 30–45 minutes; the examination does not use radiation and is considered safe in the second and third trimesters.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes congenital heart defects, which co-occur in 5–10% of gastroschisis cases and affect anaesthetic and surgical management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound probe is placed on the newborn's chest; the examination takes 20–30 minutes and requires no needles or sedation.

diagnosticTests.test: Neonatal blood tests (CBC, electrolytes, blood gas, cross-match)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess hydration, acid-base balance, and haematological status before emergency surgical repair.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn from a heel prick or umbilical line in the newborn immediately after birth.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used after repair if obstruction is suspected to identify atresia, stricture, or dysmotility that was not apparent at surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin feeding tube is placed into the bowel and a small amount of contrast is injected while X-rays are taken.

treatmentOverview: Gastroschisis is a surgical emergency; the exposed bowel must be protected immediately at birth and repaired as soon as the neonate is stable. Delivery should be planned at a centre with neonatal intensive care and paediatric surgical expertise. At birth, the bowel is immediately wrapped in warm saline-soaked gauze and cling film or a plastic bowel bag to minimise fluid losses, heat loss, and contamination. Intravenous access, nasogastric decompression, and volume resuscitation are started simultaneously. Two surgical approaches are used. Primary closure involves manually reducing the bowel and closing the fascia and skin in a single operation; it is feasible when the bowel is not too edematous and the abdominal domain is sufficient, and it is associated with shorter total parenteral nutrition (TPN) dependence. When primary closure would cause abdominal compartment syndrome, a staged repair using a preformed silo — a plastic tube sutured to the defect — allows gradual reduction of the bowel over three to seven days by gravity and gentle compression before formal closure. Both approaches are equivalent in long-term outcomes for simple gastroschisis. Non-operative 'sutureless' closure, in which the bowel is reduced and the umbilical ring allowed to contract as the wound granulates, has emerged as a safe option in selected cases and may reduce anaesthetic exposure. After closure, nutrition is maintained via central TPN while gut function recovers. Enteral feeds are introduced cautiously once stooling begins and bowel sounds return, a process that typically takes two to eight weeks for simple cases. Complex gastroschisis with atresia may require resection and staged anastomosis, significantly prolonging the course. Long-term follow-up addresses growth, nutritional status, and gastrointestinal function.

medicalTreatments.name: Immediate bowel protection and fluid resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: At birth, the bowel is wrapped in a non-adherent dressing and the infant given intravenous fluids to replace the large evaporative and third-space losses; this stabilises the infant for safe anaesthesia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential — without initial stabilisation, mortality from hypothermia and circulatory collapse approaches 100%; with it, over 90% of infants survive to surgical repair.

medicalTreatments.name: Total parenteral nutrition (TPN)
medicalTreatments.description: Centralline-delivered nutrition maintains growth and healing during the weeks before full enteral feeds can be established; lipid emulsions, amino acids, dextrose, electrolytes, vitamins, and trace elements are tailored to neonatal requirements.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents malnutrition in essentially all infants; median TPN duration is 3–5 weeks for simple gastroschisis and can extend to months in complex cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (commonly ampicillin and gentamicin) are given perioperatively and continued while a central line and open defect are present, to prevent wound infection and sepsis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard practice in all major centres; line-associated infection rates vary by institution but prophylaxis measurably reduces early sepsis events.

medicalTreatments.name: Prokinetic agents and bowel motility support
medicalTreatments.description: Agents such as erythromycin are sometimes used postoperatively to stimulate intestinal motility and hasten the transition to enteral feeds in infants with prolonged dysmotility.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence for benefit is modest; a Cochrane review found inconsistent effects on time to full enteral feeds, but short courses are used in many centres.

medicalTreatments.name: Human milk and enteral feeding progression
medicalTreatments.description: Maternal breast milk or donor human milk is introduced by nasogastric tube once bowel function begins, starting at minimal trophic volumes and advancing gradually; it is associated with lower rates of necrotising enterocolitis and infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Human milk reduces infection and NEC risk compared with formula in premature and surgical neonates; time to full feeds varies from 2 weeks in simple cases to 3–6 months in complex cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Primary fascial closure
surgicalOptions.description: The herniated bowel is reduced and the abdominal wall fascia and skin are closed in a single operation, typically under general anaesthesia in the first hours of life.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful in approximately 60–70% of simple gastroschisis cases; associated with faster recovery of gut function than staged repair.

surgicalOptions.name: Staged silo reduction
surgicalOptions.description: A prefabricated spring-loaded silo is applied to the defect at the bedside without anaesthesia; the bowel is progressively reduced over 3–7 days and formal closure performed once reduction is complete.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective for cases unsuitable for primary closure; overall survival is equivalent to primary closure, with similar time to full feeds in most series.

surgicalOptions.name: Sutureless (umbilical cord) closure
surgicalOptions.description: The bowel is reduced and the defect closed using the base of the umbilical cord as a flap or allowing the wound to epithelialise; performed without sutures under minimal or no anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Growing evidence base; several series report survival and time to full feeds equivalent to surgical closure, with a potential reduction in anaesthetic exposure.

surgicalOptions.name: Intestinal resection and anastomosis
surgicalOptions.description: Required when a segment of bowel is atretic, necrotic, or perforated; the diseased segment is excised and either immediate anastomosis or stomas are constructed depending on bowel viability.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Necessary in approximately 15–20% of cases (complex gastroschisis); associated with prolonged TPN dependence and risk of short-bowel syndrome.

alternativeTreatments.name: Fetal surgery (experimental)
alternativeTreatments.description: Intrauterine repair of gastroschisis has been attempted in a small number of experimental series to limit amniotic fluid exposure of the bowel; it remains investigational and is not standard practice.

alternativeTreatments.name: Amnioinfusion or amniotic fluid exchange
alternativeTreatments.description: Replacing amniotic fluid to reduce its protein content and chemical injury to the exposed bowel has been studied in animal models and small human series; results are inconclusive and this approach is not in routine clinical use.

whySeeSpecialist: Gastroschisis requires immediate involvement of a paediatric or neonatal surgeon with experience in abdominal wall defects. Prenatal diagnosis should prompt transfer of care to a tertiary centre combining maternal-fetal medicine, neonatal intensive care, and paediatric surgery. Centres performing higher volumes of repairs consistently report shorter TPN duration, fewer complications, and better long-term outcomes than low-volume centres. Long-term follow-up by a multidisciplinary intestinal rehabilitation team is warranted for infants with complex gastroschisis or short-bowel syndrome.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a paediatric surgeon with documented experience in neonatal abdominal wall defects. The delivery hospital should have a level-III neonatal intensive care unit, access to paediatric anaesthesia, and an intestinal rehabilitation programme for complex cases. Ask about the centre's annual volume of gastroschisis repairs and their rates of primary versus staged closure, as these reflect institutional experience. Families may also benefit from a genetics consultation to receive accurate recurrence-risk counselling.
preventionStrategies: Avoid smoking, recreational drugs, and unnecessary NSAID use in the first trimester.
Optimise pre-pregnancy nutrition and weight; underweight women planning pregnancy benefit from nutritional support.
Avoid aspirin and ibuprofen in the first trimester; use paracetamol for pain relief when needed.
Attend first-trimester antenatal booking appointments to enable early AFP screening and second-trimester ultrasound.
Genetic counselling for families with one affected child, though recurrence risk is low (approximately 3–4%) and the condition is usually sporadic.
lifestyleModifications: Parents should receive psychological support; a diagnosis of gastroschisis during pregnancy is deeply stressful, and specialist neonatal social work and psychology services should be offered.
Establish breastfeeding or milk expression early — human milk is the preferred feed after surgery and reduces infection risk.
Learn to recognise signs of feeding intolerance in the recovering infant: abdominal distension, bile-stained vomiting, or failure to stool.
Prepare for a prolonged neonatal intensive care stay; simple gastroschisis averages 3–6 weeks in hospital, complex cases much longer.
Ensure regular outpatient follow-up in the first two years to monitor growth, nutritional status, and gastrointestinal function.

recommended: Exclusive human milk (maternal or donor) for enteral feeds when available — associated with lower NEC rates in surgical neonates
Gradual enteral feed advancement guided by clinical signs of tolerance, usually starting at 10–20 mL/kg/day
High-calorie formulas or breast milk fortification for infants not gaining weight adequately after discharge
Oral motor stimulation during TPN to support feeding readiness and reduce aversion
avoid: Early aggressive enteral feeding before bowel function is established — risks aspiration and NEC
Cow's milk protein formula in the early period if human milk is available, given the higher infection and NEC risk
High-osmolality feeds initially in infants with significant bowel loss

exerciseGuidelines: There are no specific exercise restrictions for infants and children who have had uncomplicated gastroschisis repair with adequate abdominal wall closure. In the first months, normal handling is encouraged. As the child grows, standard developmental milestones should be supported. Children with a very large scar or who had complex repairs should be reviewed by the surgeon before any contact sports, but restrictions are rarely needed after the first year.
dailyManagement: Monitor stool output and urine output daily during the recovery period as indicators of gut function and hydration.
Record weight at every clinical contact to ensure adequate nutritional intake.
Watch for signs of central line infection: fever, redness at the exit site, or unexplained deterioration.
Advance oral feeds incrementally and record the volume achieved each day.
Attend all scheduled outpatient follow-up appointments — growth faltering in the first year can indicate ongoing absorptive problems.
prognosis: Survival for isolated simple gastroschisis exceeds 90–95% in high-income centres with specialised neonatal surgical teams. The great majority of survivors achieve full enteral nutrition by four to eight weeks after surgery and are discharged within six to eight weeks. Long-term gastrointestinal outcomes are generally good: most children with simple gastroschisis grow normally, have normal bowel function, and have no dietary restrictions by school age. Complex gastroschisis — affecting approximately 10–15% of cases — carries meaningfully higher morbidity: infants with extensive atresia or necrosis may develop short-bowel syndrome, requiring prolonged or permanent parenteral nutrition and sometimes intestinal transplantation. Children with short-bowel syndrome face a long rehabilitation course measured in years. Late complications including adhesive bowel obstruction requiring reoperation affect approximately 10–15% of all survivors. Cryptorchidism occurs in up to 15% of male patients and is addressed electively. Psychological and developmental outcomes are generally normal for uncomplicated survivors, though prolonged neonatal hospitalisation is a recognised source of parental stress and developmental delay risk.
recoveryTimeline: In the immediate neonatal period, surgical repair is performed within the first 24 hours. For simple gastroschisis with primary closure, bowel function typically returns over two to four weeks; full enteral feeds are usually established by three to five weeks, after which the infant is discharged, typically around six weeks of age. Staged silo repairs add approximately one week before closure but do not substantially prolong the post-closure recovery. Complex cases with atresia or short bowel may remain inpatient for months. After discharge, outpatient follow-up continues through infancy to monitor nutrition and growth. Developmental follow-up is recommended for infants who had prolonged NICU stays.
complications: Short-bowel syndrome — the most serious long-term complication, occurring when extensive bowel resection leaves insufficient absorptive length; it requires long-term parenteral nutrition.
Adhesive small-bowel obstruction — affects 10–15% of survivors and may require reoperation; presenting signs are bilious vomiting and abdominal distension.
Central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) — a major cause of morbidity during the TPN phase; prevention relies on strict sterile technique and early transition to enteral feeds.
Abdominal compartment syndrome after primary closure — raised intraabdominal pressure impairs renal perfusion and ventilation; if recognised, the closure is converted to staged repair.
Cryptorchidism — undescended testes in up to 15% of males, requiring elective orchidopexy in the first year of life.
Necrotising enterocolitis — a risk particularly in premature infants with gastroschisis; human milk and careful feed advancement are the main preventive strategies.

supportResources.name: Children's Digestive Health and Nutrition Foundation — Gastroschisis Resources
supportResources.description: Patient and family guidance on gastroschisis diagnosis, surgery, and recovery from the paediatric GI specialty society.

supportResources.name: NHS — Gastroschisis
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview for families covering what gastroschisis is, how it is treated, and what to expect after surgery.

supportResources.name: CDC — Facts About Gastroschisis
supportResources.description: Epidemiological data and factsheet on gastroschisis from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

sources.title: Overcash RT et al. Gastroschisis: A Review. Obstetrics and Gynecology, 2021
Gamba P, Midrio P. Abdominal wall defects: prenatal diagnosis, newborn management and long-term outcomes. Seminars in Pediatric Surgery, 2014
South AP et al. Gastroschisis: current surgical management. Pediatric Surgery International, 2013
Brantberg A et al. Fetal gastroschisis — risk factors and postnatal outcome. Acta Obstetricia et Gynecologica Scandinavica, 2004
European surveillance of congenital anomalies — EUROCAT

confusedWithConditions.name: Necrotising enterocolitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NEC develops postnatally in premature infants and is not a wall defect; gastroschisis is present at birth with bowel outside the body through a paraumbilical opening.

confusedWithConditions.name: Omphalocele (atrioventricular septal defect used as proxy)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Omphalocele is a midline defect at the umbilical cord base with a covering sac and a high rate of chromosomal anomalies; gastroschisis is right-sided, without a sac, and chromosomally normal in >95% of cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hirschsprung disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hirschsprung disease causes functional obstruction from absent ganglion cells; it does not produce an abdominal wall defect, though both can cause neonatal intestinal obstruction.

relatedConditions.name: Hirschsprung disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are congenital intestinal conditions presenting in the neonatal period; Hirschsprung disease causes functional obstruction that can complicate gastroschisis management if the two co-occur.

relatedConditions.name: Atrial septal defect
relatedConditions.relevance: Congenital heart defects occur in approximately 5–10% of gastroschisis cases and are assessed routinely by echocardiography after birth.

relatedConditions.name: Patent ductus arteriosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Prematurity, which is common in gastroschisis, increases PDA prevalence; both conditions may require management in the same NICU admission.

relatedConditions.name: Necrotising enterocolitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Premature infants with gastroschisis face elevated NEC risk; human milk and careful feed advancement are the main preventive strategies shared by both conditions.

faqs.question: What is gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: Gastroschisis is a birth defect where the intestines protrude through an opening in the right side of the abdominal wall, just beside the umbilical cord, without any protective covering. It affects about 4–5 in every 10,000 babies.

faqs.question: Is gastroschisis detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Over 80% of cases are diagnosed prenatally on routine second-trimester ultrasound, which shows free-floating bowel loops outside the abdomen without a sac. Elevated maternal AFP in blood screening may prompt the ultrasound assessment.

faqs.question: How is gastroschisis treated?
faqs.answer: Surgery is performed within the first 24 hours of birth. The bowel is either reduced and the abdominal wall closed in one operation (primary closure), or gradually replaced over several days using a plastic silo before formal closure. Total parenteral nutrition maintains nutrition until the bowel works.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: Survival exceeds 90–95% for simple gastroschisis at experienced neonatal surgical centres. Complex cases involving bowel atresia or necrosis carry higher morbidity, including risk of short-bowel syndrome, but most infants survive.

faqs.question: What causes gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: The exact cause is not fully known. Leading theories point to disruption of a small blood vessel in the developing abdominal wall around the fourth to fifth week of pregnancy. Risk factors include young maternal age, smoking, recreational drug use, and NSAID use in the first trimester.

faqs.question: What is the difference between gastroschisis and omphalocele?
faqs.answer: Gastroschisis is a right-sided defect with no sac covering the exposed bowel, a normally inserted umbilical cord, and a low rate of chromosomal abnormalities. Omphalocele is a midline defect at the umbilical cord base, covered by a sac, and carries a high rate of chromosomal and cardiac anomalies.

faqs.question: Will my baby with gastroschisis need long-term care?
faqs.answer: Most infants with simple gastroschisis are discharged within six to eight weeks and grow normally. Complex cases with intestinal atresia may need months in hospital, long-term nutritional support, and, rarely, intestinal transplantation for severe short-bowel syndrome.

faqs.question: Can gastroschisis happen again in a future pregnancy?
faqs.answer: The condition is usually sporadic. The recurrence risk in a subsequent pregnancy is approximately 3–4%, slightly above the general population rate of 0.04–0.05%. Genetic counselling is recommended after an affected pregnancy.

faqs.question: What is a silo in gastroschisis treatment?
faqs.answer: A silo is a pre-made plastic tube applied to the opening in the abdomen. It holds the intestines above the body and uses gravity and gentle pressure to gradually return them to the abdomen over several days before formal surgical closure.

faqs.question: Is gastroschisis painful for the baby?
faqs.answer: Infants with gastroschisis receive anaesthesia and analgesia throughout the surgical repair. Pain is managed in the neonatal intensive care unit after surgery. The immediate risk is not pain but fluid loss, heat loss, and bowel injury from exposure.

faqs.question: Can the bowel be permanently damaged in gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: The bowel is exposed to amniotic fluid throughout pregnancy, causing a progressive inflammatory injury called 'peel'. In simple cases this resolves after repair. In complex cases, bowel may be so damaged that a segment must be removed, risking short-bowel syndrome.

faqs.question: When should a mother with a gastroschisis pregnancy deliver?
faqs.answer: Most guidelines recommend planned delivery between 36 and 37 weeks at a tertiary centre with paediatric surgery and neonatal intensive care, balancing the risk of late-pregnancy bowel compromise against prematurity. Earlier delivery may be indicated if bowel dilation worsens on serial ultrasound.

faqs.question: Does gastroschisis affect brain development?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated gastroschisis does not directly affect brain development. However, prematurity and prolonged NICU stay carry some risk of developmental delay. Follow-up developmental assessments are recommended, and most children with simple gastroschisis develop normally.

faqs.question: What nutritional support does a baby with gastroschisis need?
faqs.answer: Before the bowel is working, the baby receives all nutrition intravenously (total parenteral nutrition). Once the bowel begins to function, human milk is introduced in small volumes by tube and gradually increased. Most infants are fully fed by mouth or tube within three to five weeks for simple cases.

faqs.question: Are there any adult-onset problems from having had gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: Most adults who had simple gastroschisis as newborns have normal gut function and quality of life. Late bowel obstruction from adhesions can occur in roughly 10–15% of survivors and may need treatment. Those with short-bowel syndrome from complex gastroschisis face ongoing management into adulthood.

faqs.question: Can gastroschisis be prevented?
faqs.answer: There is no guaranteed prevention, but risk can be reduced: avoid smoking and recreational drugs, do not take ibuprofen or aspirin in the first trimester, and optimise nutrition before pregnancy. Attend routine antenatal appointments for early detection.

faqs.question: What is complex gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: Complex gastroschisis is when the herniated bowel has additional problems such as atresia (a blocked segment), volvulus (twisting), or necrosis (dead tissue). It occurs in about 10–15% of cases and requires more extensive surgery with longer recovery and higher risk of short-bowel syndrome.

faqs.question: How long will my baby be in hospital for gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: Simple gastroschisis: typically three to six weeks in hospital after surgical repair. Complex gastroschisis with intestinal atresia or short-bowel complications: often several months, sometimes longer. Your surgical team will set a goal based on when your baby is feeding fully and gaining weight.

faqs.question: What support is available for families of babies with gastroschisis?
faqs.answer: Most major neonatal centres have social workers, psychologists, and family support nurses. The NICU team can connect families with peer support groups. Online organisations such as the Congenital Abdominal Wall Defect community forums provide parent-to-parent support.

faqs.question: Does gastroschisis cause growth problems?
faqs.answer: Growth is normal in the great majority of children with simple gastroschisis. Complex cases with short-bowel syndrome may have growth faltering requiring specialised nutritional support. Most children who were affected by simple gastroschisis reach normal height and weight by school age.

metaDescription: Gastroschisis is a birth defect where intestines protrude outside the abdomen. Learn causes, surgical treatment, and outcomes for this congenital condition.
